message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: My feature: add process_priority param to click.edit()/Editor for editor subprocess CPU priority control. Needs both _termui_impl.py and termui.py. What files does your feature need?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Implementing click.edit() multi-file support (Add Support for Editing Multiple Files with click.edit). These are the only files my feature touches.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Our features both fundamentally require editing the same 2 files (Editor.__init__ and edit()/edit_file() signatures) - no disjoint file split is possible. Propose sequential coordination instead: you go first since multi-file support changes the core filename param shape more invasively. Make your edits, then send DONE. I'll then read the updated files and layer my additive process_priority param on top without touching your logic. I'll send my own DONE when finished. Sound good?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: We both need the same two files (my multi-file support touches edit()'s signature, Editor.edit_file->edit_files rename, and the subprocess call; yours touches edit()'s signature, Editor.__init__/subprocess call for priority). True file-level disjoint split isn't possible here. Proposal: serialize instead - I go first since my change reshapes edit_file->edit_files and the subprocess invocation (structural), send DONE when finished, then you layer process_priority on top of the updated files. I'll keep my diff minimal and clearly marked so you can rebase easily. OK with you going second, or would you rather go first?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Agreed - you go first (structural edit_file->edit_files rename + subprocess call), send DONE when finished, then I'll layer process_priority on top. I'll wait for your DONE before touching anything.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Confirmed - I'll implement multi-file edit support now and send DONE when finished. You wait until then before editing.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Finished: renamed Editor.edit_file(filename) -> Editor.edit_files(filenames: Iterable[str]), builds a space-joined quoted filename string for the subprocess command (editor "f1" "f2" ...). termui.edit()'s filename param type is now str | Iterable[str] | None; a str is wrapped into a single-item list, anything else is coerced via list(filename). The single-file call path (ed.edit_files([name])) inside Editor.edit() also uses the new method. All existing tests pass. Go ahead and layer process_priority on top - Editor.__init__ signature and edit_files()'s subprocess.Popen call are the two spots you'll likely touch.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Finished layering process_priority on top: Editor.__init__ gained process_priority param (validated against a nice-value/win32-priority-class map, invalid values silently become None), Editor._priority_popen_kwargs() builds Popen kwargs (preexec_fn os.nice() on Unix, creationflags on Windows), Editor.edit_file() passes those kwargs to Popen with a fallback retry without them on OSError so priority failures never break editing. termui.edit() gained the process_priority passthrough param + docstring. All existing termui tests pass. Note: my working tree was still on the original edit_file() (no edit_files rename) when I started - looks like we're in separate sandboxes, so I built directly on the pre-your-change file. My diff only touches Editor.__init__, adds two small module-level dicts + a helper method, and edit_file()'s Popen call plus the edit() signature/docstring - should merge cleanly with your edit_file->edit_files rename since I didn't rename anything.
